evidence_quotes.quote: video games remain vulnerable to obsolescence.

paragraphs.text: In todays increasingly digital world, video games have become a dominant form of cultural expression, combining storytelling, visual artistry, music, and interactivity in ways that rival and often surpass traditional art forms. However, despite their artistic significance, video games remain vulnerable to obsolescence. Many titles become unplayable after developers or publishers shut down servers, remove digital rights management (DRM) systems, or discontinue support. This reality not only threatens consumers rights but also risks erasing a vital part of modern culture. To address this, video games must be included in t Digital Fairness Act, with explicit provisions ensuring their playability even after reaching end-of-life (EOL). Video games, like films, literature, or music, are works of art. They are crafted by teams of artists, writers, musicians, and developers who create experiences that resonate emotionally and intellectually with players. Games such as Journey, The Last of Us, or Gris are celebrated for their artistic visuals and powerful narratives, while titles like The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild are recognized for their innovative design and immersive worlds. Denying future generations access to these cultural landmarks because of expired licenses or server shutdowns is akin to locking away classic films or burning books once a studio ceases distribution. Unfortunately, this is not a hypothetical concern. Many games have already vanished in practice because of their reliance on online services or DRM. For instance, The Matrix Online, a massively multiplayer online game (MMO) tied to the popular film franchise, was permanently shut down in 2009. Similarly, City of Heroes, a beloved superhero MMO, was closed in 2012 despite a passionate community. Even single-player games have been affected: Ubisofts Assassins Creed Liberation HD was temporarily delisted in 2022, with fears that purchased copies would become unplayable. The closure of Halo 2 servers on the original Xbox Live network rendered its multiplayer component unplayable, erasing a significant piece of gaming history. A Digital Fairness Act that includes video games must address the right to preservation and continued playability. Consumers purchase digital games with the expectation that they will retain access, but publishers often treat these purchases as temporary licenses. When servers shut down, or when DRM verification becomes impossible, players lose access to content they have legally bought. This is a direct violation of digital ownership principles and undermines trust between developers and players. By requiring publishers to provide server emulation tools, offline modes, or DRM-free patches when a game reaches EOL, legislation could ensure games remain accessible to those who own them. Moreover, the preservation of video games is essential for cultural and historical reasons. The medium is still relatively young, but it has already seen a rich evolution of styles, genres, and technologies. Shutting down games without means of preservation risks leaving major gaps in the historical record. Consider Overwatch 1, which was permanently replaced by Overwatch 2 in 2022, making the original game a title that shaped competitive multiplayer shooters unplayable forever. Without proactive preservation measures, future researchers, historians, and fans will lose access to important pieces of digital culture. Critics may argue that enforcing post-EOL accessibility places undue burden on developers or publishers. However, practical solutions exist. When a game is no longer commercially viable, companies could be required to release server code to trusted preservation groups or to implement offline modes. Projects like fan-run servers for Star Wars Galaxies or Toontown Online demonstrate that communities are eager to keep games alive when companies will not. Legislation can formalize these practices, ensuring that such efforts are legal
